summary: McKenzie shares his first 48 hours of Google AdWords results for Bingo Card Creator, breaking down two ad groups: one targeting people directly searching for bingo card creation, and another targeting elementary teachers searching for Dolch sight word lists. He reveals his early click-through rates, cost-per-click strategies, and ad copy variations. The post demonstrates his nascent but already sharp thinking about keyword arbitrage — finding underpriced search terms that reach his exact customer. He's testing multiple ad variations and setting up conversion tracking, laying the groundwork for the data-driven marketing approach he'd later become known for.
key_points: Two distinct ad groups target different customer intents: direct product searches at $0.20/click vs. niche educational terminology at $0.04-0.10/click
Keyword arbitrage exists because competitors bid on obvious terms ('sight words') but not domain-specific synonyms ('Dolch words') that teachers actually search for
Ad copy that speaks the customer's specific language outperforms generic ads, yielding click-through rates several multiples above industry average
Google's automatic optimization of ad variations means testing multiple versions costs nothing extra while generating valuable data
The gambling industry inflates costs for any keyword containing 'bingo', creating a pricing distortion that savvy niche marketers can work around
money_quotes: Surprisingly, folks trying to sell to first grade reading teachers don't speak First Grade Reading teacher.
They bid up 'sight words' but not 'Dolch words' despite the fact that the two are synonymous to my customers.
There is money in gambling, which drives up the price of any string involving 'bingo', but there is a lot less money in teaching first graders to read.

Dolch is an education bigwig from the 50s who came up with the eponymous list of 220 most common English words, and if you're not an elementary reading teacher you will NEVER hear his name in your life but if you are he has name recognition approaching that of Einstein.
If you prep more than you teach, try out a better way for free.
What, you thought teachers actually stayed home in the summer? Ha, ha, ha, good one.
key_insight: The biggest marketing advantage for niche software comes from speaking your customer's specialized language better than competitors do, finding underpriced keywords that map perfectly to customer intent.
